story_title: Strait of Hormuz closure and Iran crisis
event_summary: Iran closed or threatened closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid US-Israel military pressure, triggering global energy-supply anxiety, diplomatic interventions by China and Turkey, and emergency rerouting of LNG tankers.
tldr: Headlines divide into four clear registers: military-escalation alarm (US/UK/Israel/India leading), energy-rerouting pragmatism (Japan/Hungary/Brazil/Egypt), Trump-Xi summit diplomacy (Canada/South Korea/Vietnam), and Iranian counter-framing (China/Iran state/Turkey). The Polish-Baltic bucket stands furthest from the corpus centre with a catastrophe-framing headline that uses threat-multiplication language absent elsewhere. Japan's headline is the corpus outlier on the economic register — the only title naming a specific cargo type and route.

evidence.quote: Iran Must Only Succeed Once to Trigger a Catastrophe
US-Israel attacks on Iran could resume as early as next week
Trump Preps Big Iran Strikes With Epic Fury 2.0
Japan set to receive first LNG via Hormuz
Air Travel Continues Normally despite Fuel Price Surge
Egyptian farmers struggling to survive as Iran war
Trump and Xi exchange praise at meeting
Trump Seeks Chinese Leverage to End Iran War
Trump says Xi agrees Iran must open strait
Iran says Hormuz fallout responsibility lies with
China's U.N. ambassador criticizes US Hormuz resolution
Blockade of Strait of Hormuz not in common interests

isolation_top.note: Catastrophe-framing headline uses threat-multiplication language ('Must Only Succeed Once') absent from all other buckets.
Only headline naming a specific cargo type and route (LNG via Hormuz); purely logistical framing diverges from geopolitical corpus centre.
French-language France Info titles in corpus create language-distance artefacts alongside substantive UN-process framing.
Domestic-continuity headline ('Air Travel Continues Normally') inverts the crisis frame dominant across the corpus.
Food-security angle (Egyptian farmers) is the only agricultural-impact headline in the corpus; diverges from military-diplomatic centre.

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Headline focused on Trump domestic political gaffes rather than Hormuz — no geopolitical frame in the title.
Headlines covered unrelated international items; no Hormuz-specific framing surfaced in titles.
Southeast Asia regional outlet led with counter-drone efforts — no Hormuz or Iran headline present.

bottom_line: Headline frames split across four registers — military alarm, energy rerouting, Trump-Xi diplomacy, and Iranian counter-framing — with the Polish-Baltic bucket furthest from the corpus centre via uniquely catastrophist language. The economic register is under-represented in titles relative to the body-text corpus, suggesting outlets led with geopolitics and buried supply-chain impacts below the fold.
